blocks.body: Prepositions and Phrasal Verbs shows you how to choose prepositions for time, place, and movement, work with fixed combinations, and use phrasal verbs more confidently in everyday English.

{"level":"A1-B2","intro":"In this section you will see how prepositions help you talk precisely about time, place, and movement, and how phrasal verbs work in everyday situations.","rules":[{"label":"Time, place, movement","color":"emerald","text":"Start with the basic at / on / in system, then move to direction and movement: to, into, onto, through, across.","example":"at 7 o'clock / on Monday / in July / walk into the room"},{"label":"Dependent prepositions","color":"blue","text":"Next, focus on fixed combinations such as good at, listen to, and reason for, and learn them as ready-made patterns.","example":"afraid of spiders / wait for a bus / a solution to the problem"},{"label":"Phrasal verbs","color":"amber","text":"In the second half of the branch you will see that the particle after the verb can be literal or idiomatic and often changes the meaning of the whole expression.","example":"pick up the keys / look after a child / run out of milk"}]}

blocks.heading: 1. What this section covers
blocks.body:
The first Prepositions and Phrasal Verbs branch stays flat and practical: one top-level category and seven direct lessons in a natural order.

  1. Prepositions of Time - at, on, in for clock times, days, dates, months, and years.
  2. Prepositions of Place - the inside, surface, and point idea behind in, on, at.
  3. Prepositions of Movement - to, into, onto, through, across, along, towards and other movement patterns.
  4. Dependent Prepositions - adjective + preposition, verb + preposition, noun + preposition.
  5. Phrasal Verbs Basics - what verb + particle combinations are and why the meaning is not always literal.
  6. Separable and Inseparable Phrasal Verbs - object position and pronoun rules.
  7. Common Phrasal Verbs by Topic - practical sets for home, study, work, and communication.

blocks.heading: 2. What to watch for
blocks.body:
• The same preposition can work differently depending on the core idea: point, surface, inside, direction, or contact.
  • In many combinations, the preposition cannot be guessed from literal translation, so dependent prepositions are best learned as chunks.
  • In phrasal verbs, the particle often changes the meaning of the whole expression: take off and take on are no longer just take.
  • When a phrasal verb is separable, a pronoun almost always goes between the verb and the particle: turn it off.
  • Short practical examples matter here because it is easier to remember a whole pattern than an isolated word.
